studies.title: [Digital therapeutics in Neurology].
studies.abstract: Since 2020 physicians and psychotherapist in Germany can prescribe digital interventions. The interventions have to meet safety and data privacy requirements and must prove their effectiveness. Eight interventions are available in the field of neurology: Two for multiple sclerosis (elevida, levidex), three for insomnia (HelloBetter Schlafen, somnio, somnovia), one for aphasia (neolexon Aphasie), one for mild cognitive impairment (NeuroNation MED), and one for migraine headache prophylaxis (sinCephalea - Migräneprophylaxe). In Germany, digital interventions can be prescribed to outpatients and as a post-hospital treatment.

_verification.reason: The abstract explicitly mentions 'sinCephalea - Migräneprophylaxe' as a digital intervention for migraine headache prophylaxis, which matches the product name and description provided. The study is not generic and specifically lists this product among other named interventions.

_classification.reason: The study discusses the availability and prescription of digital interventions in real-world settings without mentioning randomization, blinding, or interventional design. It appears to focus on observational evidence and practical application rather than controlled trials.

studies.title: A Digital Therapeutic Allowing a Personalized Low-Glycemic Nutrition for the Prophylaxis of Migraine: Real World Data from Two Prospective Studies.
studies.abstract: Migraine is a headache disorder associated with a high socioeconomic burden. The digital therapeutic sinCephalea provides an individualized low-glycemic diet based on continuous glucose measurement and is intended to provide a non-pharmacological migraine prophylaxis. We performed two prospective studies with migraine patients who used sinCephalea over a period of 16 weeks. The patients used a headache diary and recorded their migraine-related daily life impairments using the assessment tools HIT-6 and MIDAS for a pre versus post comparison. In addition, continuous glucose data of patients were compared to healthy controls. In both studies, patients reported a reduction of headache and migraine days as well as reductions in HIT-6 and MIDAS scores. More specifically, migraine days decreased by 2.40 days (95% CI [-3.37; -1.42]), HIT-6 improved by 3.17 points (95% CI [-4.63; -1.70]) and MIDAS by 13.45 points (95% CI [-22.01; -4.89]). Glucose data suggest that migraine patients have slightly increased mean glucose values compared to healthy controls, but drop into a glucose range that is below one's individual standard range before a migraine attack. In conclusion, sinCephalea is a non-pharmacological, digital migraine prophylaxis that induces a therapeutic effect within the range of pharmacological interventions.

_verification.reason: The study explicitly mentions 'sinCephalea' by name multiple times in the title and abstract, and describes its functionality and intervention in detail, which matches the provided DTx product information. The study is clearly focused on this specific digital therapeutic product.

_classification.reason: The study is observational and uses real-world data from two prospective studies without randomization, blinding, or placebo control. It focuses on pre versus post comparisons and real-world patient-reported outcomes.
